section.title: Healing the Leper and the Centurion
section.sectionRationale: Having taught with authority, Jesus now acts with authority. Matthew gathers a first set of healings: a leper cleansed by a touch, a Gentile centurion's servant healed by a word, Peter's mother-in-law raised from fever, and crowds delivered at evening. The section shows the King's power and willingness, and roots His healing ministry in Isaiah's Servant who bears our diseases.
section.thesis: The King who taught with authority now heals with authority and compassion, cleansing the untouchable, honoring the faith of an outsider above all Israel, and bearing the infirmities of His people as Isaiah's Suffering Servant, so that His power and His willingness to save are displayed together.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the leper cleansed by Jesus' touch; the centurion's great faith and the warning of a great reversal; Peter's mother-in-law healed; and the evening healings that fulfill Isaiah 53.

verses.text: When Jesus came down from the mountain, large crowds followed Him.
Suddenly a leper came and knelt before Him, saying, “Lord, if You are willing, You can make me {{katharizo|clean}}.”
Jesus reached out His hand and touched the man. “I am willing,” He said. “Be clean!” And immediately his leprosy was cleansed.
Then Jesus instructed him, “See that you don’t tell anyone. But go, show yourself to the priest and offer the gift prescribed by Moses, as a testimony to them.”
When Jesus had entered Capernaum, a centurion came and pleaded with Him,
“Lord, my servant lies at home, paralyzed and in terrible agony.”
“I will go and heal him,” Jesus replied.
The centurion answered, “Lord, I am not worthy to have You come under my roof. But just say the word, and my servant will be healed.
For I myself am a man under {{exousia|authority}}, with soldiers under me. I tell one to go, and he goes, and another to come, and he comes. I tell my servant to do something, and he does it.”
When Jesus heard this, He marveled and said to those following Him, “Truly I tell you, I have not found anyone in Israel with such great {{pistis|faith}}.
I say to you that many will come from the east and the west to share the banquet with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven.
But the sons of the kingdom will be thrown into the outer darkness, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.”
Then Jesus said to the centurion, “Go! As you have believed, so will it be done for you.” And his servant was healed at that very hour.
When Jesus arrived at Peter’s house, He saw Peter’s mother-in-law sick in bed with a fever.
So He touched her hand, and the fever left her, and she got up and began to serve Him.
When evening came, many who were demon-possessed were brought to Jesus, and He drove out the spirits with a word and healed all the sick.
This was to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah: “He took up our {{astheneia|infirmities}} and carried our diseases.”

katharizo.gloss: To cleanse, make clean, purify. Leprosy made a person ceremonially unclean and untouchable; Jesus does not merely heal but cleanses, and stunningly He touches the leper, so that His holiness overcomes the defilement rather than being defiled by it.

exousia.gloss: Authority, right, power. The centurion understands authority: as his word commands soldiers, so Jesus' word commands sickness. He sees that Jesus need only speak, recognizing a power over disease that answers to Him as troops answer to a commander.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, reliance. Jesus marvels at the centurion's faith, greater than any in Israel, a trust that simply takes Jesus at His word. Matthew highlights that such faith, found in a Gentile, foreshadows the nations streaming into the kingdom.

astheneia.gloss: Weakness, infirmity, sickness. Matthew applies Isaiah 53:4 to Jesus' healings: He took up our infirmities. His power over disease flows from His mission to bear our brokenness, a mission that reaches its climax when He bears our sin on the cross.

basic.context: Jesus comes down from the mountain and immediately begins to heal. A leper, an outcast forbidden to be touched, kneels and trusts Jesus' willingness; Jesus touches him and makes him clean. A Roman centurion, a Gentile, asks Jesus only to say the word, and Jesus marvels at faith greater than any in Israel. He heals Peter's mother-in-law, then delivers and heals crowds at evening, fulfilling Isaiah's word about the Servant who bears our diseases.
basic.hermeneutics: Read these miracles as demonstrations of the authority Jesus just claimed in the Sermon, now shown in deed. Notice the pattern: Jesus is both able and willing, and He reaches the untouchable and the outsider. The quotation of Isaiah 53:4 tells us how to understand His healings: they flow from His mission as the Suffering Servant who takes our brokenness upon Himself.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus does not just heal the leper but makes him clean, overcoming the defilement with His holiness. The centurion grasps authority: as his word moves soldiers, Jesus' word moves disease. Jesus marvels at his faith, a simple trust that takes Him at His word. And Matthew says Jesus took up our infirmities, our weakness and sickness, fulfilling Isaiah's Servant.
basic.typology: Jesus cleansing the leper fulfills what the law could only diagnose; where Leviticus quarantined the unclean, Jesus removes uncleanness. The centurion coming from outside points to the nations sharing the banquet with Abraham. And the healings are signs of the Servant of Isaiah 53, who bears the diseases and, ultimately, the sins of His people.
basic.christ: Jesus is the holy One whose touch cleanses rather than being contaminated, the King whose mere word heals at a distance, and the Servant who carries our infirmities. He is willing as well as able. And He is the Lord of the messianic banquet, who welcomes believing outsiders and warns presumptuous insiders.
basic.god: God's power and compassion meet in Jesus' healings. The God who gave the law that excluded the leper now draws near in His Son to cleanse him. He honors faith wherever He finds it, even in a Gentile soldier, and He is bringing a kingdom that gathers people from east and west while it will exclude the presumptuous.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 13 and 14 give the law of leprosy behind the cleansing. Isaiah 53:4 is quoted, the Servant who bears our infirmities. Isaiah 25 pictures the banquet of the nations on God's mountain. And Matthew 15 shows another Gentile whose faith Jesus commends.
basic.oneStory: The King who spoke with authority now acts with authority and mercy. He cleanses the untouchable, heals at a word, and welcomes the faith of an outsider, warning that the kingdom will overturn every presumption. And Matthew tells us the meaning of it all: these healings are the first fruits of the Servant who takes our infirmities upon Himself, a mission that runs straight to the cross.

context.literary: Chapters 8 and 9 gather a series of miracles that display in action the authority Jesus taught in chapters 5 to 7. This first triad, leper, centurion's servant, Peter's mother-in-law, touches an outcast Jew, a Gentile, and a woman, showing the reach of His mercy. The evening summary and the Isaiah quotation interpret the whole ministry as the work of the Suffering Servant.
context.historical: Leprosy, a term covering various skin diseases, brought ceremonial uncleanness and social exile under Leviticus 13. A centurion commanded about a hundred soldiers; a Gentile officer approaching a Jewish teacher, and confessing unworthiness, was remarkable. The banquet with the patriarchs draws on the Jewish hope of a messianic feast, which Jesus says will include surprising guests.
context.canonical: Matthew reads the healings through Isaiah 53:4, tying Jesus' ministry to the Servant Songs. The great reversal, outsiders welcomed and sons of the kingdom excluded, develops the prophetic vision of the nations coming to Zion, Isaiah 25:6-9, and anticipates the mission to all peoples that closes the Gospel, Matthew 28:19.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To demonstrate the authority and compassion of the King in action, to show that He is both willing and able to save the unclean and the outsider, and to interpret His healing ministry as the work of Isaiah's Suffering Servant who bears our infirmities.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The miracles describe Jesus' unique authority and are not a promise that every believer will be healed as these were. The centurion's faith and the leper's humble trust are held up as examples. The Isaiah quotation grounds a doctrine: Christ's healing and, ultimately, His atoning work address the whole ruin of sin.

debates.question: What does it mean that Jesus fulfilled Isaiah 53:4, He took up our infirmities?

views.label: A present sign that points to full healing at the resurrection
views.case: Jesus' healings are a genuine down payment of the Servant's saving work, real foretastes of the day when all disease and death are undone, though complete bodily healing awaits the resurrection, not the present age.

views.label: Bodily healing secured in the atonement itself
views.case: Some argue that because the Servant bore our sicknesses, physical healing is provided in Christ's atoning work and may be claimed by faith now; the text ties His healings directly to bearing our diseases.

debates.bearing: Both affirm that Christ addresses the whole misery of sin, body and soul; the wiser reading holds the healings as sure signs whose fullness comes with the resurrection, guarding against false guarantees while magnifying His saving work.

debates.question: What is the point of the centurion's faith and the warning about the sons of the kingdom?

views.label: Faith, not ethnicity, marks the kingdom's true members
views.case: A Gentile's trust surpasses Israel's, and Jesus foretells many coming from east and west, teaching that entry to the kingdom rests on faith in Him, not physical descent from Abraham.

views.label: A sober warning against religious presumption
views.case: The sons of the kingdom cast into darkness are those who presume on privilege without faith; the scene warns every insider that heritage without trust in Christ ends in exclusion.

debates.bearing: Both underline that the kingdom belongs to those who believe; the outsider's faith is welcomed and the insider's presumption is judged, foreshadowing the gospel to all nations.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus does not merely cure the leper but makes him katharizo, clean, and does so by touching him, His holiness overcoming the man's defilement rather than being stained by it. The centurion, schooled in exousia, authority, sees that Jesus need only speak, for disease obeys His word as soldiers obey a command. Jesus marvels at the man's pistis, a faith greater than any in Israel, a bare trust that takes Him at His word. And Matthew interprets the evening healings by Isaiah 53:4: Jesus took up our astheneia, our weakness and sickness, the Servant bearing our brokenness on His way to bearing our sin.

typology.type: The leper cleansed by a touch
typology.fulfillment: What the law could only diagnose and quarantine, Leviticus 13, Jesus removes, cleansing the unclean by His holy touch.
The centurion's faith foreshadows the nations coming from east and west to feast with the patriarchs, Isaiah 25:6-9.

typology.type: The Servant who bears our diseases
typology.fulfillment: Isaiah 53:4 fulfilled as Jesus takes up our infirmities, His healings a sign of the atonement to come.

study.christ: These verses reveal Jesus as the holy King whose power and mercy are perfectly joined. His touch cleanses the leper instead of being defiled; His word heals a servant across the town without His moving a step; His hand lifts a fevered woman to service. He is both able and, wonderfully, willing, I am willing, be clean. He is Lord of the coming banquet, welcoming a believing Gentile and warning presumptuous heirs. And Matthew unveils the deepest truth: every healing flows from His identity as the Suffering Servant who takes our infirmities upon Himself, the same love that will carry our sins to the cross.
study.god: In Jesus' works we see the heart of God: holy yet drawing near to the unclean, powerful yet moved by compassion. The God who once quarantined the leper now comes in His Son to cleanse him; the God of Israel honors the faith of a Roman soldier and promises a kingdom gathered from every direction. He is no respecter of mere pedigree; He welcomes faith and warns presumption. And He is the God who does not save from a distance but takes our weakness upon Himself, bearing our diseases and our sins to make us whole.

crossReferences.note: The leper's uncleanness and exile under the law, reversed by Jesus' cleansing touch.
The Servant who took up our infirmities and carried our diseases, quoted of Jesus' healings.
The banquet on God's mountain for all peoples, echoed in the feast with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.
Another Gentile, the Canaanite woman, whose great faith Jesus commends.

study.oneStory: The King who spoke with authority now shows that same authority over disease, distance, and defilement, and joins it to a tender willingness to save. He cleanses the outcast, heals at a word, and marvels at an outsider's faith, announcing a kingdom that overturns every presumption and gathers people from east and west. Above all, Matthew tells us why He heals: He is the Servant who takes our infirmities upon Himself. The road that begins with a touch of mercy for a leper runs on to the cross, where He will bear not only our diseases but our sins.

quiz.q: What is striking about how Jesus healed the leper?

options.text: He touched the untouchable man and made him clean, His holiness overcoming the defilement.
options.note: Verse 3 shows Jesus touching the leper and cleansing him, reversing the law's quarantine.

options.text: He healed the leper from a distance with only a word.
options.note: That describes the centurion's servant; the leper Jesus actually touched.

options.text: He sent the leper to the priest before any healing occurred.
options.note: The cleansing came first by Jesus' touch; then He sent him to the priest as a testimony.

options.text: He declared that the leper was never really unclean.
options.note: The man was genuinely unclean; Jesus cleansed him, He did not deny the condition.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus marvel at the centurion?

options.text: His faith, trusting Jesus to heal by a word, was greater than any Jesus had found in Israel.
options.note: Verse 10 records Jesus marveling at faith unmatched in Israel.

options.text: He was a powerful Roman officer who humbled himself.
options.note: His humility is real, but Jesus specifically marvels at his faith, not merely his rank or humility.

options.text: He had memorized the law of Moses despite being a Gentile.
options.note: Nothing is said of his law-keeping; Jesus commends his faith in Jesus' word.

options.text: He offered a large payment for the healing.
options.note: No payment is mentioned; it was his faith that Jesus praised.

quiz.q: What did Jesus foretell about the messianic banquet?

options.text: Many will come from east and west to feast with the patriarchs, while presumptuous heirs are cast out.
options.note: Verses 11-12 announce the great reversal of outsiders welcomed and insiders excluded.

options.text: Only the descendants of Abraham will share the banquet.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus says many from the nations will come, while some heirs are shut out.

options.text: The banquet is only a symbol with no real gathering.
options.note: Jesus speaks of a real gathering in the kingdom, not a mere symbol.

options.text: No Gentile could ever enter the kingdom of heaven.
options.note: Jesus explicitly welcomes believing outsiders to the feast.

quiz.q: How does Matthew interpret Jesus' healings?

options.text: As fulfilling Isaiah 53:4, the Servant who took up our infirmities and carried our diseases.
options.note: Verse 17 ties the healings to the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53.

options.text: As proof that Jesus was a uniquely gifted physician.
options.note: They show His divine authority, but Matthew roots them in the Servant's saving mission, not medical skill.

options.text: As unrelated signs with no deeper meaning.
options.note: Matthew gives them profound meaning by citing Isaiah, not treating them as random.

options.text: As a guarantee that no believer will ever be sick.
options.note: The healings are signs of the Servant's work; they do not promise no believer will ever be ill.

quiz.q: What does the whole section reveal about Jesus?

options.text: He is both able and willing to save, reaching the unclean and the outsider as the Servant who bears our brokenness.
options.note: The healings display His power, His willingness, and His Servant mission together.

options.text: He is a powerful healer chiefly interested in large crowds.
options.note: He does heal crowds, but the passage centers on His willing mercy and Servant identity, not crowd size.

options.text: He heals only respectable, religious insiders.
options.note: The opposite; He cleanses a leper and heals a Gentile's servant, reaching outsiders.

options.text: He is reluctant to help and must be persuaded each time.
options.note: He readily says I am willing and I will go; His willingness is emphasized.

questions: The leper said, Lord, if You are willing, and Jesus answered, I am willing. Where do you doubt not God's power but His willingness to help you, and how does Jesus' answer speak to that?
The centurion simply trusted Jesus' word. In what situation is God calling you to take Him at His word rather than needing to see or control the outcome?
Jesus warned that heritage without faith leads to exclusion, while an outsider's faith was welcomed. Where might you be presuming on background or religion instead of trusting Christ?
Peter's mother-in-law, once healed, got up and served. How does the healing and grace you have received move you to serve Jesus and others?
Jesus took up our infirmities on His way to bearing our sins. How does knowing your Savior carries your brokenness change the way you bring your weaknesses to Him?

translationNotes.note: A leper was one afflicted with a skin disease under Leviticus 13, rendering a person ceremonially unclean and socially excluded.
Servant may also be rendered child, or servant-boy, as the BSB footnote notes; the centurion pleads for a member of his household.
The quotation is Isaiah 53:4, from the fourth Servant Song, which Matthew applies to Jesus' healing ministry as the bearing of our infirmities.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 8:1-17. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; poem, line-break, and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
